Active Work Architecture
─[ Operator Procedure Overlays and Dynamic Rule Loading ]─ ┐

Operator Procedure Overlays and Dynamic Rule Loading

Why this exists

Operators need a safe, structured way to express deployment-specific operating preferences without overloading session-start context or relying only on root instruction files.

This brainstorm explores a model where Lore can:

  • recognize operator-specific procedures (style, process, preferences)
  • load only the rules needed for the current task
  • keep a small always-on profile for persistent persona preferences

Core concept

Treat the homepage/dashboard as the control center for operator-curated operating procedures.

Instead of pushing every rule into session-start context, Lore loads:

  1. Always-on baseline rules (small, stable)
  2. Dynamic on-demand rules selected by task intent and workflow stage

Rule classes

1) Always-on profile

Small set loaded every session, for stable preferences that should always apply.

Examples:

  • preferred nickname/address style
  • communication voice/persona preference
  • non-negotiable interaction constraints

2) Workflow behavior rules

Operational rules loaded when specific task categories are active.

Examples:

  • commit message conventions and release note style
  • testing expectations before commit/push
  • PR template preferences by repository type

3) Domain overlays

Procedure packs for specific domains/environments.

Examples:

  • homelab operations conventions
  • production safety restrictions
  • enterprise security posture requirements

4) Incident/temporary overlays

Short-lived rules activated for current incidents or temporary modes.

Examples:

  • freeze window rules
  • high-risk change window escalation
  • migration-mode restrictions

Registry model

Use a procedure registry/directory so Lore knows:

  • what rules exist
  • when each rule should trigger
  • precedence and conflict resolution
  • whether the rule is always-on or dynamic

This enables discovery-first behavior: Lore sees metadata first, then loads detailed rule content only when needed.

Possible metadata schema (conceptual)

  • id
  • title
  • scope (global, repo, deployment, operator)
  • mode (always_on, dynamic)
  • triggers (keywords, command classes, lifecycle stages)
  • precedence
  • conflicts_with
  • status (active, paused)

UX direction

Dashboard integration for procedure overlays is explored in the Dashboard Homepage brainstorm. If both concepts are promoted, the dashboard becomes the primary management surface for procedure packs.

Safety and governance considerations

  • keep personal/sensitive details out of shared/public repos
  • separate persona/style preferences from safety-critical operational rules
  • require explicit confirmation for high-impact dynamic rules
  • keep a concise activation log for postmortem and trust

Relationship to current Lore model

This idea complements existing session-start instructions and docs by adding a controlled, dynamic layer:

  • session-start remains stable foundation
  • procedure registry provides adaptive behavior
  • operator retains direct control over preferences without context bloat

Open questions

  • What is the minimum always-on payload that still feels personalized?
  • Should dynamic load be keyword-driven, command-class driven, or both?
  • How should precedence work when operator rules conflict with framework safety defaults?
  • Should procedure packs be versioned and promotable across tiers?
  • What should be visible to end users vs internal to agent execution logs?

Potential next step if promoted

Promote this brainstorm into a plan that defines:

  • registry structure and storage location
  • trigger engine and precedence model
  • homepage/dashboard UX and editing flow
  • safety guardrails and audit behavior
  • migration path from ad-hoc instruction files
